title: Lesson 31 — The Rivalry

words.definition: to compete or struggle to achieve something; also, to assert
words.example: Six teams contend for the single trophy each year.

words.definition: to compete eagerly with others for something
words.example: The two firms vied for the same contract.
She studied her adversary's every past match.

words.definition: a person who actively opposes or is hostile to another
words.example: The senator became the chief antagonist of the bill.

words.definition: a long-standing rival or a source of inevitable downfall
words.example: The detective finally cornered his old nemesis.

words.definition: to prevent someone from accomplishing something; to frustrate
to prevent a plan or attempt from succeeding
words.example: A tip-off foiled the robbery at the last minute.

words.definition: to seize power or a position wrongfully or by force
words.example: The general tried to usurp the throne.

words.definition: to take the place of, especially by force or scheming
words.example: The upstart hoped to supplant the reigning champion.

words.definition: to prove more powerful; to win out
words.example: After a long fight, the underdog prevailed.
The army vanquished its rivals in a single afternoon.

words.definition: inspiring fear or respect through being impressively powerful
words.example: She was a formidable opponent in any debate.

story: For years the two champions would [[contend|contend]] for the same title, forced to [[vie|vie]] in every tournament. Each was the other's fiercest [[adversary|adversary]], a natural [[antagonist|antagonist]] and, in time, a genuine [[nemesis|nemesis]]. One would [[thwart|thwart]] the other's strategy, then [[foil|foil]] a comeback in the final round; each dreamed of the day he might [[usurp|usurp]] the other's crown and [[supplant|supplant]] him for good. Only one could [[prevail|prevail]] and [[vanquish|vanquish]] so [[formidable|formidable]] a rival — and no one could say in advance which it would be.
